question: What are the design advantages of having a vacuum inside the CNV during normal operation?

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system piping, specifically focusing on steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the primary degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that the NuScale design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not introduce additional risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear remaining the most likely degradation mechanism and secondary side corrosion risks assessed as low.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module's Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It details the integration of these components within the reactor vessel, emphasizing the steam generator system's structure and its role in maintaining the pressure boundaries between the primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, and leakage rates are monitored through RCS inventory balance and comparison with the containment evacuation system (CES) flow rate, with operational limits set in technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. It interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure boundary to prevent the release of fission products and provides structural support for various reactor components, with all materials and fabrication processes adhering to strict ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure long-term integrity.

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel, providing a critical barrier to fission product release. The RPV and its components are constructed from materials specified by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code to ensure structural integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design life.

edges.description: Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B discusses the design and operational experience of reactor coolant system piping, both of which are connected through their focus on ensuring the integrity and safety of the reactor coolant system under normal and abnormal conditions.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards for overpressure protection that must be met, while Section B describes the physical components and design features of the reactor coolant system that are subject to those codes.

Section A outlines the regulatory and code compliance requirements for the design and integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B discusses the practical challenges of detecting leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both of which are connected through their focus on maintaining the integrity and monitoring of the reactor coolant system.

Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system components, including the steam generators, while Section A specifies detailed design considerations and operational experience for the steam generator tubing, connecting the general system description to specific design advantages such as reduced risk of foreign object wear.

Section A discusses design features and risks related to reactor coolant system components, while Section B addresses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, with both sections connected by their focus on the integrity and monitoring of the reactor coolant system during operation.

Section A describes the design of the reactor coolant system, including the containment vessel (CNV), while Section B discusses leakage detection challenges within the same system, both of which are connected by the containment's role in managing and detecting coolant boundary integrity, relevant to the analysis of vacuum advantages.

Section A describes system features (such as degasification and venting to the CNV) that help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is the focus of the regulatory compliance requirements outlined in Section B.

Section A discusses the removal of noncondensible gases and maintaining system integrity via vacuum conditions in the CNV, while Section B addresses steam generator tube wear and foreign object risks, with both sections relating to the operational environment inside the CNV but not directly connecting vacuum conditions to SG tube design advantages.

Section A describes the function of the degasification line and vent valves in removing noncondensible gases from the pressurizer, while Section B lists these components (including degasification vent lines) as part of the RCS design, showing that the system features in A are specified as part of the overall subsystem design in B.
Section A describes the design features of the reactor coolant system, which are subject to the overpressure protection and code compliance requirements outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the overall design of the NuScale reactor coolant system, which includes the use of natural circulation and an integral design, while Section B discusses specific degradation mechanisms in steam generator tubing and compares the NuScale design to existing PWRs, thus Section B provides detailed component-level considerations that specify and elaborate on the general system described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design basis, while Section B specifies the detailed components and subsystems that implement the design described in Section A, including the reactor pressure vessel and associated systems.

Section A describes the degasification and venting features of the RCS that remove noncondensible gases, which is facilitated by having a vacuum in the CNV during normal operation as part of the overall RCS design described in Section B.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor and containment vessels, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that the design, including the containment vessel (CNV), must comply with, thus Section A's design details are subject to the requirements specified in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor vessel and its relationship to the containment vessel (CNV), while Section B specifies the detailed components and subsystems housed within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) as referenced in Section A.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor and containment vessels, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR) that govern the design and construction of these vessels, connecting them through the requirement that the vessel materials and configuration in A must comply with the codes specified in B.

Section A provides a general overview of the containment vessel (CNV) and its relationship to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B specifies the internal components and subsystems housed within the RPV, thereby detailing the design context referenced in Section A.

Section A and Section B both provide identical general descriptions of the NuScale Power Module's configuration, specifically noting that the containment vessel (CNV) surrounds the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is relevant background for understanding the context of a vacuum inside the CNV.
